taxRateNote: 2025 municipal mill rate (Class 1 residential). The City's 2025-2029 Financial Plan confirms a typical municipal tax bill of $3,081 on a $787,743 average BC Assessment value for 2025. Municipal portion only — total tax bill also includes school, regional district, library, hospital, and BC Assessment levies. BC publishes rates as mills (per $1,000) which we’ve converted to a % for direct comparison.
typicalHomeValueNote: Average 2025 BC Assessment value for Class 1 residential, as published in the City's 2025-2029 Financial Plan. Worth noting: BC home values are 2-3× NB equivalents, which is why Nanaimo's effective rate looks low even though the absolute tax bill ($3,081) is comparable to peer cities.
sourceLabel: City of Nanaimo 2025-2029 Financial Plan (adopted by Council May 2025)
dataStatusNote: Transcribed from the City of Nanaimo 2025-2029 Financial Plan, 2025 Operating Expenditures by Department table. Worth noting how Nanaimo differs structurally: (1) public safety is much higher (38.7%) than NB/AB peers because Community Safety includes Fire + Bylaw + Parking, plus RCMP is a separate $36.6M line; (2) Nanaimo bundles Waterworks ($14.7M) and Sanitary Sewer ($4.6M) directly into the operating budget — other cities run these as separately-funded utilities not shown in operating; (3) debt service is embedded in departmental costs (the City reports it as small Internal Debt Repayment allocations rather than a standalone Fiscal Services line).

nativeDepartments.name: Community Services — Community Safety (Nanaimo Fire Rescue, Bylaw Enforcement, Parking, emergency management)
Community Services — RCMP (168-member contract budgeted at 92% of projected cost)
Engineering & Public Works (roads, traffic, fleet, solid waste, drainage/stormwater, cemeteries, engineering services)

Note: unlike Moncton/Saint John/Fredericton/Lethbridge, Nanaimo includes water and sewer utility expenses directly in its operating budget rather than running them as a separate utility fund. Funding still primarily comes from user fees on the utility bill.
Community Services — Parks, Recreation & Culture (parks, rec facilities, cultural operating grants — Port Theatre, Nanaimo Museum, Nanaimo Art Gallery)
Planning & Development (Community Planning, Sustainability, Business Licensing & Permit Centre, Current Planning, Subdivision, Development Engineering, Environmental Protection, Building Inspections)
Corporate Services (Finance, Information Technology, Customer Service, internal support)
City Administration (CAO's Office, Human Resources, Legislative Services, Communications)

Nanaimo embeds debt service in departmental costs rather than breaking it out as a separate line. Internal Debt Repayment shows as a $0.5M allocation in the budget summary; outstanding debt covers Fire Stations #1 and #4, the Vancouver Island Conference Centre, solid-waste collection trucks, and the Water Treatment Plant.

Operating grants to cultural venues (Port Theatre, Nanaimo Museum, Nanaimo Art Gallery) are bundled inside Community Services — Parks, Recreation & Culture. There's no separate Grants line in this budget.
